title: Enhanced $s^\pm$-wave superconductivity in electron-doped La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$
authors: Xun Liu, Chao Deng, Wenfeng Wu, Liang Si, Mi Jiang
abstract: In cuprates, electron doping yields a much lower superconducting $T_c$ than hole doping. For recently discovered nickelate superconductors, the analogous doping strategies become more challenging. Consequently, while hole-doped Ruddlesden-Popper (RP) nickelates have been extensively studied, electron-doped RP nickelates remain rarely explored both experimentally and theoretically. Here we fill this gap by systematically investigating the two-orbital bilayer model for three representative systems: bulk La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ at ambient pressure and 15\,GPa, and a heterostructure La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$:La$_3$Al$_2$O$_7$ that provides a feasible experimental route to electron doping. Using first-principle calculations and large-scale dynamical cluster quantum Monte Carlo simulations, we find that electron doping generically enhances $s^\pm$-wave pairing superconductivity (SC) in all three cases, with the heterostructure showing the highest $T_c$ in the underdoped regime. Furthermore, our results suggest an inter-orbital cooperative mechanism that the pairing on the $d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbital, induced by that on the $d_{z^2}$ orbital, plays vital role in the SC. This work provides the first theoretical prediction of enhanced SC in electron-doped RP nickelates and calls for future experimental verification.
summary: This paper provides the first theoretical prediction that electron doping enhances superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates. It identifies a cooperative mechanism between d_{x2-y2} and d_{z2} orbitals and suggests that heterostructures could achieve higher transition temperatures. This work offers a new direction for experimentalists seeking to control superconductivity in nickelate-based materials.
main_problem: Investigating the effect of electron doping on the $s^\pm$-wave superconducting pairing in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates, specifically addressing the lack of research on the electron-doped regime compared to the hole-doped regime.
main_result: Electron doping generically enhances $s^\pm$-wave superconductivity in bulk La3Ni2O7 and LaNi(Al)O heterostructures, driven by a cooperative mechanism where pairing on the d_{x2-y2} orbital is induced by the d_{z2} orbital.
method: The study uses first-principles DFT to obtain hopping parameters and large-scale Dynamical Cluster Approximation (DCA) with a continuous-time quantum Monte Carlo (CT-QMC) solver to solve the Bethe-Salpeter equation.
model_or_system: A bilayer two-orbital tight-binding model on a 2D square lattice representing La3Ni2O7 at 0 GPa, 15 GPa, and a La3Ni2O7:La3Al2O7 heterostructure, including intra- and inter-orbital Coulomb interactions and Hund's coupling.
key_observables: Superconducting transition temperature (Tc), s+/--wave pairing symmetry, pair-field susceptibility, and orbital-resolved pairing components.
important_parameters: Electron doping level (delta), pressure (0 GPa vs 15 GPa), Hubbard U (4.0 eV), inter-orbital U' (2.4 eV), and Hund's coupling J (0.8 eV).
main_assumptions: The DCA method approximates long-range physics via a mean-field bath, and the self-energy is assumed to be constant within each Brillouin zone patch.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the bilayer two-orbital model; Figure 4 illustrates the temperature dependence of the BSE eigenvalue for different doping levels under various pressure/heterostructure conditions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of electron doping in nickelates, describes the DFT-derived two-orbital model and DCA methodology, presents results on Tc enhancement and orbital-resolved pairing, and concludes with the proposed inter-orbital cooperative mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the bilayer two-orbital model on a square lattice. The model incorporates three types of hoppings: (1) intralayer intra-orbital hoppings: nearest-neighbor (txx/zz 1 ), next-nearest-neighbor (txx/zz 2 ), and next-next-nearest-neighbor (txx/zz 3 ); (2) intralayer inter- orbital hopping (txz); and (3) interlayer hoppings: intra- orbital (txx/zz ⊥ ) and inter-orbital (txz ⊥). The values of these parameters for 0 Gpa, 15 Gpa and LaNi(Al)O are listed in Table.S1 in Supplementary [11].
FIG. 2. Extrapolated Tc as a function of electron doping level δ for three different cases with the Nc = 4 × 2 cluster.

FIG. 3. Characteristic BSE eigenvectors revealing stronger interlayer pairing for two distinct cases at their lowest accessible temperatures: (left) LaNi(Al)O with electron doping level δ = 0.1 at T = 0.06 eV; (right) 15 GPa with δ = 0.3 at T = 0.09 eV. The label lm denotes orbital l in layer m.

FIG. 4. Temperature dependence of the eigenvalue 1 −λs± for extrapolating Tc under three distinct conditions: 15 GPa (circles) with doping levels δ = 0.0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4; and 0 GPa (squares) and LaNi(Al)O (triangles) both with δ = 0.0, 0.1, 0.2.

FIG. 5. (a) Temperature dependence of the inverse of s±- wave pair-field susceptibility P −1(T) for the 15 GPa and LaNi(Al)O cases under different electron doping levels δ. The inset shows the corresponding extrapolated Tc obtained from P −1(Tc) = 0; (b) Orbital-resolved components (P x Γ and P z Γ) of the interacting part of s±-wave pair-field susceptibility at δ = 0.0, 0.2, and 0.4 for both cases; (c) Characteristic temperature evolution of the weights η (defined in the main text) for the orbital-resolved intralayer (ϕxx/zz ∥ ) and interlayer
